urn:contentItem:6JJF-5SK3-S8FH-X16K-00000-00SC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-off2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:22ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSSC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-offNicole Blevins

When the wind is blowing in North Myrtle Beach, small-scale turbine blades spin in the breeze at three beach-access locations.

These turbines are not powering cities, spanning miles and standing hundreds of feet in the air. But they are part of South Carolina's plan to test wind energy potential, a plan that has been in the works for almost two decades.

Today South Carolina is home to one of the most advanced wind turbine testing facilities in the world and has years of mapping and feasibility studies under its belt, yet it is still one of six states in the U.S. without usable wind power generation.

In 2008, South Carolina Act 318 created a committee staffed by the state energy office to study and make recommendations for wind capability in the state. The study intended to understand the state's wind potential to determine its investment worth, and the committee submitted 18 recommendations to promote future projects.

Over the years, dozens of involved scientists, economic strategists and local governments joined to put motions into place by studying the economic opportunity, mapping sea floors and critical habitat and testing wind potential at different heights. Mainly funded by federal grants, the studies took hundreds of thousands of dollars, and researchers poured years of their time without knowing their progress would eventually be halted.

Dr. Paul Gayes, Coastal Carolina University executive director for the Burroughs and Chapin Center for marine and wetland studies, has spent his career studying renewable energies and the effects of carbon in the atmosphere. He's been part of the wind conversation from the start, but has seen other states jump on the opportunity before South Carolina.

"South Carolina wasn't slow necessarily," Gayes said. "It just takes forever to do things."

Now nearly 20 years later, the state sits with the same potential and no finish line in sight.

Cost of South Carolina energy and the need for renewables

Offshore wind energy started in the northeast where the resources and cost of energy were higher, but as technology has evolved, cost and resources in the southeast became less of an issue positioning the region for a better chance at success.

Gayes said initial investment for offshore wind energy can be costly, but onshore farms could be built tomorrow and be economically competitive.

Santee Cooper residential customers consumed over 2 million megawatt hours (MWh) in 2024 , and Dominion Energy residential customers consumed nearly 9 million MWh in 2025 . The average South Carolina residential customer paid $149.51 a month for power in 2024, and $155.14 in 2025, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

"We are an area that is growing at an enormously rapid rate," Gayes said. "The demand for power is going up."

In 2024, about half of South Carolina's energy generation came from nuclear plants, which don't release carbon or greenhouse gases, but nuclear is not a renewable energy source as it relies on uranium extraction and poses challenges with radioactive waste management. Only about 4% came from solar and hydrokinetic power, and the rest came from coal, natural gas and oil.

Considering the impact the state has already seen from a global carbon issue, wind energy has become a cost-effective way in other regions to reduce carbon emissions.

But the tug-of-war of political power and desire for renewable energy at the federal level has stunted progress nationwide, leaving South Carolina's potential for pay-off still up in the air.

History of wind energy in the state

The southeast is the last region in the country to develop any sort of wind energy dependence. It's mainly due to the lack of resources with the technology that was available. But South Carolina and nearby states started to play catch up in the 2000s during the country's push for renewable energy solutions.

One of the recommendations of a 2008 state feasibility study was to develop a Regulatory Task Force for Coastal Clean Energy, studying the potential for wind, wave and tidal energy development. Leaders of the task force wanted to know what a wind energy industry would look like for South Carolina's economy and if current regulations could foster an industry.

Initial modeling and reports suggested offshore wind farms could bring a significant amount of jobs, particularly with construction crews, engineers and mechanics.

In 2009, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded $45 million to Clemson University to develop a full-scale wind turbine testing facility using drive-train technology. Another $53 million of state and private donations led to its opening in 2013. It's now one of the most advanced turbine testing facilities in the world and serves a global market.

Clemson declined to be interviewed for the story.

Proponents of offshore wind pointed to the facility as an enormous asset for South Carolina's ability to transport the turbines due to its port location at the former U.S. Naval Base in North Charleston.

While recognition of offshore potential was slower statewide, one city welcomed the idea from the start.

North Myrtle Beach and Santee Cooper constructed a few small turbines, mainly under the guise of public awareness. The effort was to introduce locals to a technology they weren't familiar with and to educate about the economic benefits of wind.

Members of the North Strand Coastal Wind Team, Monroe Baldwin, former NMB Chamber of Commerce head of economic development; Greg Duckworth, former NMB councilman; and Gayes, partnered with the city to build an advocacy for wind energy in the area. Public outreach was a huge contributor to the support that was lacking in the rest of the state.

The City of North Myrtle Beach had a proclamation to become a wind powered city, Baldwin said, and he promoted the idea of using the turbines as a tourism opportunity for fishing charters, diving and sightseeing.

"North Myrtle Beach is the only coastal community that said 'Yes,'" Baldwin said. "Everybody else up and down the coast around us said, 'We don't want to see them.'"

Researchers continued the process of mapping areas of the state's coast and testing wind speeds, all steps that needed to occur before offshore wind leases could be granted. The Palmetto Wind Project, led by Gayes as an initial offshore study for Santee Cooper, found that winds get better as you go offshore and higher, and in the state they get better from south to north.

"Off the Grand Strand, there actually is, that was known at that time, a healthy potential for wind," Gayes said.

The area was also well-positioned for offshore leases because of the shallow depths of the area's outer continental shelf. If sea floors were deeper, offshore turbines would need high-scale floating systems to keep them stabilized, which cost an exorbitant amount to build. However, every mile further offshore raised the cost to build even traditional towers, Gayes said.

"While our wind fields were not necessarily stronger than it might be, say in Massachusetts or Maine, the cumulative effect of having a much bigger area that could be cheaply realized and the character of the winds is... I think South Carolina had the potential to be one of the top wind (production) states," Gayes said.

Since the Grand Strand area's power grid is relatively new, it could take a significant amount of energy from offshore without a major overhaul like it would in other parts of the country. Habitat mapping found minimal environmental impacts. There were no significant cultural resources or important shipwreck sites in the mapping area. Things appeared to be moving in a positive direction.

But since the offshore areas available to lease were in federal waters, they required federal action, and the administration handoff in 2016 changed the outlook for renewables across the country.

How federal politics interrupted state energy plans

In 2006, the Gulf of Mexico Energy Security Act created a moratorium banning oil and gas leasing in parts of the Gulf of Mexico and was set to expire on June 30, 2022.

But at the tail end of President Donald Trump's first administration, he extended the moratorium to ban offshore leasing in North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Florida for another decade, and included offshore wind leases.

When President Joe Biden's administration took over, efforts to revitalize investment in offshore wind escalated. The U.S. Department of Energy made a goal to reach 30 gigawatts of offshore wind energy production by 2030, kickstarting the leasing processes for other offshore projects in a more ready position. (One offshore wind turbine in future U.S. projects would produce between 12 and 14 megawatts, meaning there would have to be roughly 2,000 to 2,500 operational wind turbines to reach the goal).

While South Carolina was still getting its footing, offshore wind leases were granted in North Carolina in May 2022 in an attempt to secure them before the initial moratorium deadline. The Bureau of Ocean Energy Management auctioned the leases just across the border of the Grand Strand to TotalEnergies, a French energy company, and Duke Energy.

The Biden-Era Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 included a provision for every 60 million acres of oil and gas leasing put up for auction, offshore wind leasing must also be offered to create certainty for wind availability.

When administrations changed hands again, Trump doubled down on his stance against renewable energy, revoking the ability to lease offshore wind.

His latest action consisted of canceling offshore wind leases in New York and North Carolina in March 2026 and other projects in New Jersey, New York and California in April, essentially refunding the companies what they paid for the leases plus interest.

Karly Brownfield, senior program manager of the Southeastern Wind Coalition, said when the administration sends signals implying investors can follow all the rules to get a project permitted and approved but still face an extreme risk that a project could get stopped after significant investments have been made, it is not a signal an investor wants to see.

"At the end of the day, I think the biggest impact that the Trump administration has had on the offshore wind industry, (and) the clean energy industry as a whole, is uncertainty," she said.

While the lease canceling did not directly affect South Carolina, Gayes said because of the industry's halt the state won't see offshore production for likely another 15 years.

What could be next for South Carolina wind?

With offshore wind off the table for the time being, Brownfield recognized the state's opportunity to pivot to onshore wind investment.

Onshore wind farms are not typically seen in the southeast because of its presumptive lack of wind resources. But early models of wind potential were "tremendously different" than actual speeds, according to Gayes. Evolved turbine designs and taller towers can help accommodate the region, but transportation becomes an issue.

What's different about onshore projects in the southeast versus the Midwest or western regions is building wind farms on private land, Brownfield said. Building on private land, typically on someone's farm, offers economic benefits to rural communities.

"Because they're built in rural areas, the onshore wind farm, nine times out of 10 becomes the largest taxpayer in the county," she said.

In Chowan County, North Carolina, Timbermill Wind started construction in May 2023 and began clean energy generation in December 2024. It's projected to generate $50 million in tax revenue to the county in its 30-year lifespan. The landowners themselves also receive lease payments, providing a reliable source of income.

Brownfield said after the construction period, each tower takes up less than half an acre and still leaves significant usable land, but success of the projects depends on community support.

Gayes said he was working to put together proposals for a test bed site in South Carolina to prove the capacities and economics of onshore wind potential, but the change in administration, and with it interest in wind power, has put things on hold.

The lack of support for projects like onshore and offshore wind development often comes from a rejection of the world's climate issue caused by increased carbon output, Gayes said.

As a coastal state already seeing the effects of a warming climate - increased inland flooding, beach erosion and sea-level rise - Gayes said doing nothing is not an option.

The smarter option would be to invest in all types of energy production, including oil and gas, but at varying degrees, Gayes said, but the reality of transforming an oil-based economy overnight is impractical.

"There is no magic bullet," he said.

While the future of wind in South Carolina is uncertain, the preparation is already there for when the time comes for offshore leasing, and Gayes is certain it will come.

value.Title: SC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-off

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-5SK3-S8FH-X167-00000-00SC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-off2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:22ZLexisNexisRENEWABLE ENERGY NEWSSC has studied wind energy for almost 20 years. Why there's still no pay-offNicole Blevins

When the wind is blowing in North Myrtle Beach, small-scale turbine blades spin in the breeze at three beach-access locations.

These turbines are not powering cities, spanning miles and standing hundreds of feet in the air. But they are part of South Carolina's plan to test wind energy potential, a plan that has been in the works for almost two decades.

Today South Carolina is home to one of the most advanced wind turbine testing facilities in the world and has years of mapping and feasibility studies under its belt, yet it is still one of six states in the U.S. without usable wind power generation.

In 2008, South Carolina Act 318 created a committee staffed by the state energy office to study and make recommendations for wind capability in the state. The study intended to understand the state's wind potential to determine its investment worth, and the committee submitted 18 recommendations to promote future projects.

Over the years, dozens of involved scientists, economic strategists and local governments joined to put motions into place by studying the economic opportunity, mapping sea floors and critical habitat and testing wind potential at different heights. Mainly funded by federal grants, the studies took hundreds of thousands of dollars, and researchers poured years of their time without knowing their progress would eventually be halted.

Dr. Paul Gayes, Coastal Carolina University executive director for the Burroughs and Chapin Center for marine and wetland studies, has spent his career studying renewable energies and the effects of carbon in the atmosphere. He's been part of the wind conversation from the start, but has seen other states jump on the opportunity before South Carolina.

"South Carolina wasn't slow necessarily," Gayes said. "It just takes forever to do things."

Now nearly 20 years later, the state sits with the same potential and no finish line in sight.

Cost of South Carolina energy and the need for renewables

Offshore wind energy started in the northeast where the resources and cost of energy were higher, but as technology has evolved, cost and resources in the southeast became less of an issue positioning the region for a better chance at success.

Gayes said initial investment for offshore wind energy can be costly, but onshore farms could be built tomorrow and be economically competitive.

Santee Cooper residential customers consumed over 2 million megawatt hours (MWh) in 2024 , and Dominion Energy residential customers consumed nearly 9 million MWh in 2025 . The average South Carolina residential customer paid $149.51 a month for power in 2024, and $155.14 in 2025, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

"We are an area that is growing at an enormously rapid rate," Gayes said. "The demand for power is going up."

In 2024, about half of South Carolina's energy generation came from nuclear plants, which don't release carbon or greenhouse gases, but nuclear is not a renewable energy source as it relies on uranium extraction and poses challenges with radioactive waste management. Only about 4% came from solar and hydrokinetic power, and the rest came from coal, natural gas and oil.

Considering the impact the state has already seen from a global carbon issue, wind energy has become a cost-effective way in other regions to reduce carbon emissions.

But the tug-of-war of political power and desire for renewable energy at the federal level has stunted progress nationwide, leaving South Carolina's potential for pay-off still up in the air.

History of wind energy in the state

The southeast is the last region in the country to develop any sort of wind energy dependence. It's mainly due to the lack of resources with the technology that was available. But South Carolina and nearby states started to play catch up in the 2000s during the country's push for renewable energy solutions.

One of the recommendations of a 2008 state feasibility study was to develop a Regulatory Task Force for Coastal Clean Energy, studying the potential for wind, wave and tidal energy development. Leaders of the task force wanted to know what a wind energy industry would look like for South Carolina's economy and if current regulations could foster an industry.

Initial modeling and reports suggested offshore wind farms could bring a significant amount of jobs, particularly with construction crews, engineers and mechanics.

In 2009, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded $45 million to Clemson University to develop a full-scale wind turbine testing facility using drive-train technology. Another $53 million of state and private donations led to its opening in 2013. It's now one of the most advanced turbine testing facilities in the world and serves a global market.

Clemson declined to be interviewed for the story.

Proponents of offshore wind pointed to the facility as an enormous asset for South Carolina's ability to transport the turbines due to its port location at the former U.S. Naval Base in North Charleston.

While recognition of offshore potential was slower statewide, one city welcomed the idea from the start.

North Myrtle Beach and Santee Cooper constructed a few small turbines, mainly under the guise of public awareness. The effort was to introduce locals to a technology they weren't familiar with and to educate about the economic benefits of wind.

Members of the North Strand Coastal Wind Team, Monroe Baldwin, former NMB Chamber of Commerce head of economic development; Greg Duckworth, former NMB councilman; and Gayes, partnered with the city to build an advocacy for wind energy in the area. Public outreach was a huge contributor to the support that was lacking in the rest of the state.

The City of North Myrtle Beach had a proclamation to become a wind powered city, Baldwin said, and he promoted the idea of using the turbines as a tourism opportunity for fishing charters, diving and sightseeing.

"North Myrtle Beach is the only coastal community that said 'Yes,'" Baldwin said. "Everybody else up and down the coast around us said, 'We don't want to see them.'"

Researchers continued the process of mapping areas of the state's coast and testing wind speeds, all steps that needed to occur before offshore wind leases could be granted. The Palmetto Wind Project, led by Gayes as an initial offshore study for Santee Cooper, found that winds get better as you go offshore and higher, and in the state they get better from south to north.

"Off the Grand Strand, there actually is, that was known at that time, a healthy potential for wind," Gayes said.

The area was also well-positioned for offshore leases because of the shallow depths of the area's outer continental shelf. If sea floors were deeper, offshore turbines would need high-scale floating systems to keep them stabilized, which cost an exorbitant amount to build. However, every mile further offshore raised the cost to build even traditional towers, Gayes said.

"While our wind fields were not necessarily stronger than it might be, say in Massachusetts or Maine, the cumulative effect of having a much bigger area that could be cheaply realized and the character of the winds is... I think South Carolina had the potential to be one of the top wind (production) states," Gayes said.

Since the Grand Strand area's power grid is relatively new, it could take a significant amount of energy from offshore without a major overhaul like it would in other parts of the country. Habitat mapping found minimal environmental impacts. There were no significant cultural resources or important shipwreck sites in the mapping area. Things appeared to be moving in a positive direction.

But since the offshore areas available to lease were in federal waters, they required federal action, and the administration handoff in 2016 changed the outlook for renewables across the country.

How federal politics interrupted state energy plans

In 2006, the Gulf of Mexico Energy Security Act created a moratorium banning oil and gas leasing in parts of the Gulf of Mexico and was set to expire on June 30, 2022.

But at the tail end of President Donald Trump's first administration, he extended the moratorium to ban offshore leasing in North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Florida for another decade, and included offshore wind leases.

When President Joe Biden's administration took over, efforts to revitalize investment in offshore wind escalated. The U.S. Department of Energy made a goal to reach 30 gigawatts of offshore wind energy production by 2030, kickstarting the leasing processes for other offshore projects in a more ready position. (One offshore wind turbine in future U.S. projects would produce between 12 and 14 megawatts, meaning there would have to be roughly 2,000 to 2,500 operational wind turbines to reach the goal).

While South Carolina was still getting its footing, offshore wind leases were granted in North Carolina in May 2022 in an attempt to secure them before the initial moratorium deadline. The Bureau of Ocean Energy Management auctioned the leases just across the border of the Grand Strand to TotalEnergies, a French energy company, and Duke Energy.

The Biden-Era Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 included a provision for every 60 million acres of oil and gas leasing put up for auction, offshore wind leasing must also be offered to create certainty for wind availability.

When administrations changed hands again, Trump doubled down on his stance against renewable energy, revoking the ability to lease offshore wind.

His latest action consisted of canceling offshore wind leases in New York and North Carolina in March 2026 and other projects in New Jersey, New York and California in April, essentially refunding the companies what they paid for the leases plus interest.

Karly Brownfield, senior program manager of the Southeastern Wind Coalition, said when the administration sends signals implying investors can follow all the rules to get a project permitted and approved but still face an extreme risk that a project could get stopped after significant investments have been made, it is not a signal an investor wants to see.

"At the end of the day, I think the biggest impact that the Trump administration has had on the offshore wind industry, (and) the clean energy industry as a whole, is uncertainty," she said.

While the lease canceling did not directly affect South Carolina, Gayes said because of the industry's halt the state won't see offshore production for likely another 15 years.

What could be next for South Carolina wind?

With offshore wind off the table for the time being, Brownfield recognized the state's opportunity to pivot to onshore wind investment.

Onshore wind farms are not typically seen in the southeast because of its presumptive lack of wind resources. But early models of wind potential were "tremendously different" than actual speeds, according to Gayes. Evolved turbine designs and taller towers can help accommodate the region, but transportation becomes an issue.

What's different about onshore projects in the southeast versus the Midwest or western regions is building wind farms on private land, Brownfield said. Building on private land, typically on someone's farm, offers economic benefits to rural communities.

"Because they're built in rural areas, the onshore wind farm, nine times out of 10 becomes the largest taxpayer in the county," she said.

In Chowan County, North Carolina, Timbermill Wind started construction in May 2023 and began clean energy generation in December 2024. It's projected to generate $50 million in tax revenue to the county in its 30-year lifespan. The landowners themselves also receive lease payments, providing a reliable source of income.

Brownfield said after the construction period, each tower takes up less than half an acre and still leaves significant usable land, but success of the projects depends on community support.

Gayes said he was working to put together proposals for a test bed site in South Carolina to prove the capacities and economics of onshore wind potential, but the change in administration, and with it interest in wind power, has put things on hold.

The lack of support for projects like onshore and offshore wind development often comes from a rejection of the world's climate issue caused by increased carbon output, Gayes said.

As a coastal state already seeing the effects of a warming climate - increased inland flooding, beach erosion and sea-level rise - Gayes said doing nothing is not an option.

The smarter option would be to invest in all types of energy production, including oil and gas, but at varying degrees, Gayes said, but the reality of transforming an oil-based economy overnight is impractical.

"There is no magic bullet," he said.

While the future of wind in South Carolina is uncertain, the preparation is already there for when the time comes for offshore leasing, and Gayes is certain it will come.

urn:contentItem:6JH5-1823-SDN0-G4WT-00000-00May 04, 2026: Real Estate Team Expands Dentons US' Charleston, South Carolina, Office2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:21ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 04, 2026: Real Estate Team Expands Dentons US' Charleston, South Carolina, Office

Dentons US announced today the continued expansion of its Charleston, South Carolina, office with the addition of Real Estate partners Ryan Goer and Bryan Walpole. They are joined by Real Estate counsel Steve McCrae and Caitlin Nevin, further strengthening the Firm's growing presence in the region.

The additions come just one week after Dentons US announced the opening of its Charleston office, marking another step forward in the Firm's strategic expansion into one of the fastest-growing markets in the country. The Firm sees Charleston as a key hub for supporting clients across an expanding business community, a strong regional talent pool, and industries critical to long-term economic growth.

"The pace at which we are building our Charleston office speaks to both the opportunity in this market and our confidence in its long-term potential," said Dentons US Chief Executive Officer Sonia Martin. "Real estate is a core practice for Dentons US and a key area of strategic investment. This continued expansion strengthens our ability to serve clients across the South and reinforces our commitment to strategic, sustainable growth."

Goer and Walpole advise mortgage portfolio lenders, servicers, borrowers, and private and institutional lenders on a wide range of secured lending matters. Their experience includes loan origination and the servicing of securitized loans, including defeasances, assumptions, modifications, consents, and other servicing matters. Walpole also advises on the purchase and sale of distressed debt.

McCrae and Nevin represent clients across complex commercial real estate transactions and development matters. Nevin also advises on acquisitions, dispositions, financings, leasing, and joint ventures and has particular experience in renewable energy development, retail leasing, zoning, and entitlements, including South Carolina Fee‑in‑Lieu of Tax (FILOT) agreements. McCrae focuses on governmental entitlements and economic incentives for industrial and developer clients and is a LEED Accredited Professional.

"Building depth and capability in Charleston is a key priority for the Firm," said John Holahan, Managing Partner of Dentons US. "These additions reflect our commitment to growing a high-performing office in a rapidly growing market that pairs well nationally with the strength of our leading practice groups and presence across money centers."

The Charleston office opened with a group of eight partners across multiple practices. The initial Real Estate team included Amy Ironmonger, Bo Manuel, and Matt Norton. Walker Coleman and Cecilia Ehresman joined the Employment and Labor practice, while Jamie Bruce, Ernie Cochran, and Cooper Hawley joined the Corporate practice.

value.Title: Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC , SC move forward
value.Overview: The Fayetteville Observer (North Carolina), NEWS; Pg. C4, 788words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGR-R7J3-RTFC-W4RB-00000-00Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. C4Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward

The Carolinas are uniquely positioned to be leaders in the offshore wind economy. We have the ports, the manufacturing base, and the workforce ... To see $1 billion of our own taxpayers' money used as a "stop-work" order is an insult to the workers who were expecting those jobs and the consumers facing soaring energy costs.

What if someone told you that all you have to do to get significant new clean energy on the grid, create hundreds of well-paying jobs, and catalyze significant new economic development and supply chain businesses, would be to not spend $1 billion in taxpayer dollars and instead let the private sector and free market work?

It sounds like a common-sense victory for any fiscal conservative or energy advocate. Yet, according to recent reports, some renewable energy opponents in the federal government are finalizing a plan to do the exact opposite.

Recently revealed documents show these individuals are preparing a roughly $1 billion "reimbursement" for foreign developers. The purpose of this payout? To convince them to simply walk away from major offshore wind projects, including the Carolina Long Bay project off our coast. It's a bad deal for taxpayers and the citizens of North and South Carolina.

For residents of the Carolinas, this isn't just a policy debate; it's a direct raid on our regional economic future. TotalEnergies and Duke Energy won these leases in a fair, competitive auction, collectively paying over $315 million for the right to develop. They weren't asking for a handout - they were bringing a checkbook.

The loss of the Carolina Long Bay project is a loss of scale according to research from the North Carolina Department of Commerce. If developed, the lease areas were projected to support:

Massive Energy Output: Over 1.3 gigawatts of power, enough to provide clean, reliable electricity to 500,000 homes.

Job Creation: Conservative estimates suggest the project would support more than 14,000 jobs during construction and more than 1000 long-term, high-paying technical roles during operation and maintenance.

Economic Output: According to an economic impact study, during construction the project will support employee earnings of more than$1 billion and total economic output of nearly $4 billion. During operations, it will support earnings of $79 million and annual economic output of $257 million.

Supply Chain Investment: The developers had already committed tens of millions of dollars toward domestic supply chain and workforce training. This would have trickled down to our local ports, maritime contractors, and manufacturers who are already part of the 500+ wind industry facilities across the U.S.

Instead of these benefits, the federal government plans to use $1 billion of your tax money to pay TotalEnergies to not invest in North and South Carolina. This is the antithesis of a free-market energy strategy.

In a true market-driven economy, the government shouldn't be in the business of picking winners and losers - and it certainly shouldn't be paying billions to ensure that nobody wins. This intervention would create an artificial scarcity of energy and stifle competition. Moreover, the proposal asks foreign developers to instead invest in projects that would take American natural gas and ship it overseas when we desperately need more of our own energy, not less.

Supporters of this buyout might argue it's a necessary step to protect national security. However, federal courts have already struck down five previous attempts to halt offshore wind construction, citing a lack of evidence for any such security risks. If the projects are safe and the private sector is willing to fund them, why is the government reaching for the taxpayer's wallet to stop them?

The Carolinas are uniquely positioned to be leaders in the offshore wind economy. We have the ports, the manufacturing base, and the workforce ready to go. To see $1 billion of our own taxpayers' money used as a "stop-work" order is an insult to the workers who were expecting those jobs and the consumers facing soaring energy costs.

The best deal for the taxpayer isn't a billion-dollar payout. It's private sector investment that results in a stronger, cleaner, and more independent energy future for the Carolinas.

Mark Fleming serves as President and CEO of Conservatives for Clean Energy. Reach him at mark@cleanenergyconservatives.com

Your Turn

Mark Fleming

Guest Columnist

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGR-R7J3-RTMM-5045-00000-00Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A4Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward

What if someone told you that all you have to do to get significant new clean energy on the grid, create hundreds of well-paying jobs, and catalyze significant new economic development and supply chain businesses, would be to not spend $1 billion in taxpayer dollars and instead let the private sector and free market work?

It sounds like a common-sense victory for any fiscal conservative or energy advocate. Yet, according to recent reports, some renewable energy opponents in the federal government are finalizing a plan to do the exact opposite.

Recently revealed documents show these individuals are preparing a roughly $1 billion "reimbursement" for foreign developers. The purpose of this payout? To convince them to simply walk away from major offshore wind projects, including the Carolina Long Bay project off our coast. It's a bad deal for taxpayers and the citizens of North and South Carolina.

For residents of the Carolinas, this isn't just a policy debate; it's a direct raid on our regional economic future. TotalEnergies and Duke Energy won these leases in a fair, competitive auction, collectively paying over $315 million for the right to develop. They weren't asking for a handout - they were bringing a checkbook.

The loss of the Carolina Long Bay project is a loss of scale according to research from the North Carolina Department of Commerce. If developed, the lease areas were projected to support:

Massive Energy Output: Over 1.3 gigawatts of power, enough to provide clean, reliable electricity to 500,000 homes.

Job Creation: Conservative estimates suggest the project would support more than 14,000 jobs during construction and more than 1000 long-term, high-paying technical roles during operation and maintenance.

Economic Output: According to an economic impact study, during construction the project will support employee earnings of more than$1 billion and total economic output of nearly $4 billion. During operations, it will support earnings of $79 million and annual economic output of $257 million.

Supply Chain Investment: The developers had already committed tens of millions of dollars toward domestic supply chain and workforce training. This would have trickled down to our local ports, maritime contractors, and manufacturers who are already part of the 500+ wind industry facilities across the U.S.

Instead of these benefits, the federal government plans to use $1 billion of your tax money to pay TotalEnergies to not invest in North and South Carolina. This is the antithesis of a free-market energy strategy.

In a true market-driven economy, the government shouldn't be in the business of picking winners and losers - and it certainly shouldn't be paying billions to ensure that nobody wins. This intervention would create an artificial scarcity of energy and stifle competition. Moreover, the proposal asks foreign developers to instead invest in projects that would take American natural gas and ship it overseas when we desperately need more of our own energy, not less.

Supporters of this buyout might argue it's a necessary step to protect national security. However, federal courts have already struck down five previous attempts to halt offshore wind construction, citing a lack of evidence for any such security risks. If the projects are safe and the private sector is willing to fund them, why is the government reaching for the taxpayer's wallet to stop them?

The Carolinas are uniquely positioned to be leaders in the offshore wind economy. We have the ports, the manufacturing base, and the workforce ready to go. To see $1 billion of our own taxpayers' money used as a "stop-work" order is an insult to the workers who were expecting those jobs and the consumers facing soaring energy costs.

The best deal for the taxpayer isn't a billion-dollar payout. It's private sector investment that results in a stronger, cleaner, and more independent energy future for the Carolinas.

Mark Fleming serves as President and CEO of Conservatives for Clean Energy. Reach him at mark@cleanenergyconservatives.com

Your Turn

Mark Fleming

Guest columnist

urn:contentItem:6JGR-R7J3-RTFC-W3KY-00000-00Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A9Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward

What if someone told you that all you have to do to get significant new clean energy on the grid, create hundreds of well-paying jobs, and catalyze significant new economic development and supply chain businesses, would be to not spend $1 billion in taxpayer dollars and instead let the private sector and free market work?

It sounds like a common-sense victory for any fiscal conservative or energy advocate. Yet, according to recent reports, some renewable energy opponents in the federal government are finalizing a plan to do the exact opposite.

Recently revealed documents show these individuals are preparing a roughly $1 billion "reimbursement" for foreign developers. The purpose of this payout? To convince them to simply walk away from major offshore wind projects, including the Carolina Long Bay project off our coast. It's a bad deal for taxpayers and the citizens of North and South Carolina.

For residents of the Carolinas, this isn't just a policy debate; it's a direct raid on our regional economic future. TotalEnergies and Duke Energy won these leases in a fair, competitive auction, collectively paying over $315 million for the right to develop. They weren't asking for a handout - they were bringing a checkbook.

The loss of the Carolina Long Bay project is a loss of scale according to research from the North Carolina Department of Commerce. If developed, the lease areas were projected to support:

Massive Energy Output: Over 1.3 gigawatts of power, enough to provide clean, reliable electricity to 500,000 homes.

Job Creation: Conservative estimates suggest the project would support more than 14,000 jobs during construction and more than 1000 long-term, high-paying technical roles during operation and maintenance.

Economic Output: According to an economic impact study, during construction the project will support employee earnings of more than$1 billion and total economic output of nearly $4 billion. During operations, it will support earnings of $79 million and annual economic output of $257 million.

Supply Chain Investment: The developers had already committed tens of millions of dollars toward domestic supply chain and workforce training. This would have trickled down to our local ports, maritime contractors, and manufacturers who are already part of the 500+ wind industry facilities across the U.S.

Instead of these benefits, the federal government plans to use $1 billion of your tax money to pay TotalEnergies to not invest in North and South Carolina. This is the antithesis of a free-market energy strategy.

In a true market-driven economy, the government shouldn't be in the business of picking winners and losers - and it certainly shouldn't be paying billions to ensure that nobody wins. This intervention would create an artificial scarcity of energy and stifle competition. Moreover, the proposal asks foreign developers to instead invest in projects that would take American natural gas and ship it overseas when we desperately need more of our own energy, not less.

Supporters of this buyout might argue it's a necessary step to protect national security. However, federal courts have already struck down five previous attempts to halt offshore wind construction, citing a lack of evidence for any such security risks. If the projects are safe and the private sector is willing to fund them, why is the government reaching for the taxpayer's wallet to stop them?

The Carolinas are uniquely positioned to be leaders in the offshore wind economy. We have the ports, the manufacturing base, and the workforce ready to go. To see $1 billion of our own taxpayers' money used as a "stop-work" order is an insult to the workers who were expecting those jobs and the consumers facing soaring energy costs.

The best deal for the taxpayer isn't a billion-dollar payout. It's private sector investment that results in a stronger, cleaner, and more independent energy future for the Carolinas.

Mark Fleming serves as President and CEO of Conservatives for Clean Energy. Reach him at mark@cleanenergyconservatives.com

Your Turn

Mark Fleming

Guest columnist

urn:contentItem:6JGR-R7J3-RTMM-508M-00000-00Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A12Government needs to let wind energy projects in NC, SC move forward

To convince them to simply walk away from major offshore wind projects, including the Carolina Long Bay project off our coast. It's a bad deal for taxpayers and the citizens of North and South Carolina.

For residents of the Carolinas, this isn't just a policy debate; it's a direct raid on our regional economic future. TotalEnergies and Duke Energy won these leases in a fair, competitive auction, collectively paying over $315 million for the right to develop. They weren't asking for a handout - they were bringing a checkbook.

The loss of the Carolina Long Bay project is a loss of scale according to research from the North Carolina Department of Commerce. If developed, the lease areas were projected to support:

Massive Energy Output: Over 1.3 gigawatts of power, enough to provide clean, reliable electricity to 500,000 homes.

Job Creation: Conservative estimates suggest the project would support more than 14,000 jobs during construction and more than 1000 long-term, high-paying technical roles during operation and maintenance.

Economic Output: According to an economic impact study, during construction the project will support employee earnings of more than$1 billion and total economic output of nearly $4 billion. During operations, it will support earnings of $79 million and annual economic output of $257 million.

Supply Chain Investment: The developers had already committed tens of millions of dollars toward domestic supply chain and workforce training. This would have trickled down to our local ports, maritime contractors, and manufacturers who are already part of the 500+ wind industry facilities across the U.S.

Instead of these benefits, the federal government plans to use $1 billion of your tax money to pay TotalEnergies to not invest in North and South Carolina. This is the antithesis of a free-market energy strategy.

In a true market-driven economy, the government shouldn't be in the business of picking winners and losers - and it certainly shouldn't be paying billions to ensure that nobody wins. This intervention would create an artificial scarcity of energy and stifle competition. Moreover, the proposal asks foreign developers to instead invest in projects that would take American natural gas and ship it overseas when we desperately need more of our own energy, not less.

Supporters of this buyout might argue it's a necessary step to protect national security. However, federal courts have already struck down five previous attempts to halt offshore wind construction, citing a lack of evidence for any such security risks. If the projects are safe and the private sector is willing to fund them, why is the government reaching for the taxpayer's wallet to stop them?

The Carolinas are uniquely positioned to be leaders in the offshore wind economy. We have the ports, the manufacturing base, and the workforce ready to go. To see $1 billion of our own taxpayers' money used as a "stop-work" order is an insult to the workers who were expecting those jobs and the consumers facing soaring energy costs.

The best deal for the taxpayer isn't a billion-dollar payout. It's private sector investment that results in a stronger, cleaner, and more independent energy future for the Carolinas.

Mark Fleming serves as President and CEO of Conservatives for Clean Energy. Reach him at mark@cleanenergyconservatives.com

Your Turn

Mark Fleming

Guest columnist

urn:contentItem:6JK2-3F43-SHSP-W0F4-00000-00May 06, 2026: Charleston Growth: Dentons US Welcomes Real Estate Partners Goer, Walpole, and Counsel McCrae, Nevin2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:22ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 06, 2026: Charleston Growth: Dentons US Welcomes Real Estate Partners Goer, Walpole, and Counsel McCrae, Nevin

Dentons US has added Real Estate partners Ryan Goer and Bryan Walpole and Real Estate counsel Steve McCrae and Caitlin Nevin to its Charleston, SC office, following the firm’s initial Charleston launch on 06th May 2026. Goer and Walpole bring extensive experience advising mortgage portfolio lenders, servicers, borrowers and lenders on secured lending, including loan origination and servicing of securitized loans, defeasances, assumptions, modifications and consent processes. Walpole also handles distressed debt transactions.

McCrae and Nevin will handle complex commercial real estate transactions and development matters, with Nevin specializing in renewable energy development, retail leasing, zoning and entitlements, including South Carolina Fee-in-Lieu of Tax agreements. McCrae concentrates on governmental entitlements and incentives for industrial and developer clients and is a LEED Accredited Professional.

INDEX

SECTION 1 DENTONS US LLP PROFILE

SECTION 2 DENTONS US LLP TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 DENTONS US LLP PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Dentons US LLP, part of Dentons, the worlds largest law firm, was founded in 2013 through a merger and is headquartered in Washington, D.C. With over 1,000 U.S. lawyers across 20 offices, it serves clients in technology, energy, and healthcare, specializing in corporate law, litigation, and IP. In 2024, Dentons advised on cross-border M&As and expanded its startup practice, per its recognition among top firms for SaaS companies. Its culture emphasizes global collaboration, diversity, and pro bono work. Competing with DLA Piper, Dentons stands out for its polycentric model and extensive global network. Recent initiatives include digital transformation and ESG-focused legal services.

1.2 SUMMARY

urn:contentItem:6JK4-CP13-RS50-G2GV-00000-00May 06, 2026: Dentons US Expands Charleston Office with New Real Estate Partners and Counsel2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:22ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 06, 2026: Dentons US Expands Charleston Office with New Real Estate Partners and Counsel

Dentons US announced the addition of Real Estate partners Ryan Goer and Bryan Walpole, along with Real Estate counsel Steve McCrae and Caitlin Nevin, to its Charleston, South Carolina, office. The expansion comes one week after the firm opened its Charleston office on 06th May 2026, underscoring the firm's commitment to a growing market.

Goer and Walpole bring deep experience advising mortgage portfolio lenders, servicers, borrowers, and both private and institutional lenders on secured lending matters, including loan origination and servicing of securitized loans, defeasances, assumptions, modifications, consents, and other servicing matters. Walpole also counsels on the purchase and sale of distressed debt.

McCrae and Nevin will represent clients in complex commercial real estate transactions and development matters, with Nevin possessing a background in renewable energy development, retail leasing, zoning and entitlements, including South Carolina Fee-in-Lieu of Tax agreements. McCrae focuses on governmental entitlements and economic incentives for industrial and developer clients, and is a LEED Accredited Professional.

INDEX

SECTION 2 DENTONS US LLP TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 DENTONS US LLP PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Dentons US LLP, part of Dentons, the worlds largest law firm, was founded in 2013 through a merger and is headquartered in Washington, D.C. With over 1,000 U.S. lawyers across 20 offices, it serves clients in technology, energy, and healthcare, specializing in corporate law, litigation, and IP. In 2024, Dentons advised on cross-border M&As and expanded its startup practice, per its recognition among top firms for SaaS companies. Its culture emphasizes global collaboration, diversity, and pro bono work. Competing with DLA Piper, Dentons stands out for its polycentric model and extensive global network. Recent initiatives include digital transformation and ESG-focused legal services.

value.Title: Wind project will benefit residents of NC , SC | Opinion

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGX-7CW3-RWPD-30P4-00000-00Wind project will benefit residents of NC, SC | Opinion2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisWind project will benefit residents of NC, SC | OpinionMark Fleming, Guest Columnist

What if someone told you that all you have to do to get significant new clean energy on the grid, create hundreds of well-paying jobs, and catalyze significant new economic development and supply chain businesses, would be to not spend $1 billion in taxpayer dollars and instead let the private sector and free market work?

It sounds like a common-sense victory for any fiscal conservative or energy advocate. Yet, according to recent reports, some renewable energy opponents in the federal government are finalizing a plan to do the exact opposite.

Recently revealed documents show these individuals are preparing a roughly $1 billion "reimbursement" for foreign developers. The purpose of this payout? To convince them to simply walk away from major offshore wind projects, including the Carolina Long Bay project off our coast. It’s a bad deal for taxpayers and the citizens of North and South Carolina.

For residents of the Carolinas, this isn’t just a policy debate; it’s a direct raid on our regional economic future. TotalEnergies and Duke Energy won these leases in a fair, competitive auction, collectively paying over $315 million for the right to develop. They weren't asking for a handout — they were bringing a checkbook.

Link to Image

More about wind energy: Wind farm deal off Wilmington coast canceled. Here's why.

The loss of the Carolina Long Bay project is a loss of scale according to research from the North Carolina Department of Commerce. If developed, the lease areas were projected to support:

Massive Energy Output: Over 1.3 gigawatts of power, enough to provide clean, reliable electricity to 500,000 homes.

Job Creation: Conservative estimates suggest the project would support more than 14,000 jobs during construction and more than 1000 long-term, high-paying technical roles during operation and maintenance.

Economic Output: According to an economic impact study, during construction the project will support employee earnings of more than$1 billion and total economic output of nearly $4 billion. During operations, it will support earnings of $79 million and annual economic output of $257 million.

Supply Chain Investment: The developers had already committed tens of millions of dollars toward domestic supply chain and workforce training. This would have trickled down to our local ports, maritime contractors, and manufacturers who are already part of the 500+ wind industry facilities across the U.S.

Instead of these benefits, the federal government plans to use $1 billion of your tax money to pay TotalEnergies to not invest in North and South Carolina. This is the antithesis of a free-market energy strategy.

In a true market-driven economy, the government shouldn’t be in the business of picking winners and losers — and it certainly shouldn't be paying billions to ensure that nobody wins. This intervention would create an artificial scarcity of energy and stifle competition. Moreover, the proposal asks foreign developers to instead invest in projects that would take American natural gas and ship it overseas when we desperately need more of our own energy, not less.

Supporters of this buyout might argue it’s a necessary step to protect national security. However, federal courts have already struck down five previous attempts to halt offshore wind construction, citing a lack of evidence for any such security risks. If the projects are safe and the private sector is willing to fund them, why is the government reaching for the taxpayer’s wallet to stop them?

The Carolinas are uniquely positioned to be leaders in the offshore wind economy. We have the ports, the manufacturing base, and the workforce ready to go. To see $1 billion of our own taxpayers’ money used as a "stop-work" order is an insult to the workers who were expecting those jobs and the consumers facing soaring energy costs.

The best deal for the taxpayer isn't a billion-dollar payout. It’s private sector investment that results in a stronger, cleaner, and more independent energy future for the Carolinas.

Mark Fleming serves as President and CEO of Conservatives for Clean Energy. Reach him at mark@cleanenergyconservatives.com.

This article originally appeared on Wilmington StarNews: Wind project will benefit residents of NC, SC | Opinion

value.Title: May 06, 2026: Dentons US Enhances Charleston Office with New Real Estate Partners

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-8BN3-S333-S03W-00000-00May 06, 2026: Dentons US Enhances Charleston Office with New Real Estate Partners2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:22ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 06, 2026: Dentons US Enhances Charleston Office with New Real Estate Partners

Dentons US has announced the addition of Real Estate partners Ryan Goer and Bryan Walpole, along with Real Estate counsel Steve McCrae and Caitlin Nevin, to its Charleston, South Carolina, office. This expansion comes just one week after Dentons US opened its Charleston office on 06th May 2026, marking another strategic move into one of the fastest-growing markets in the country.

Goer and Walpole bring extensive experience advising mortgage portfolio lenders, servicers, borrowers, and both private and institutional lenders on various secured lending matters. Their expertise includes loan origination and the servicing of securitized loans, covering aspects such as defeasances, assumptions, modifications, consents, and other servicing matters. Meanwhile, Walpole also provides counsel on the purchase and sale of distressed debt. McCrae and Nevin will represent clients in complex commercial real estate transactions and development matters, with Nevin possessing a strong background in renewable energy development, retail leasing, zoning, and entitlements, including South Carolina Fee-in-Lieu of Tax agreements. McCrae focuses on governmental entitlements and economic incentives for industrial and developer clients, and is a LEED Accredited Professional.

The Charleston office initially opened with a group of eight partners across multiple practices. This included a foundational Real Estate team consisting of Amy Ironmonger, Bo Manuel, and Matt Norton, alongside other attorneys specializing in Employment and Labor and Corporate practices. Dentons US perceives Charleston as a critical hub for supporting clients within an expanding business community, enhanced by a strong regional talent pool and industries vital for long-term economic growth.

Real estate is recognized as a core practice for Dentons US and a significant area of strategic investment. The ongoing buildout of the Charleston office underscores the firm's commitment to establishing a robust presence in rapidly growing regional markets, which aligns with its national practice groups and money center footprint.

Sonia Martin, Chief Executive Officer of Dentons US, remarked, 'The pace at which we are building our Charleston office speaks to both the opportunity in this market and our confidence in its long-term potential. Real estate is a core practice for Dentons US and a key area of strategic investment. This continued expansion strengthens our ability to serve clients across the South and reinforces our commitment to strategic, sustainable growth.' Additionally, John Holahan, the Managing Partner, emphasized, 'Building depth and capability in Charleston is a key priority for the Firm. These additions reflect our commitment to growing a high-performing office in a rapidly growing market that pairs well nationally with the strength of our leading practice groups and presence across money centers.'

INDEX

SECTION 1 DENTONS US LLP PROFILE

SECTION 2 DENTONS US LLP TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 DENTONS US LLP PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Dentons US LLP, part of Dentons, the worlds largest law firm, was founded in 2013 through a merger and is headquartered in Washington, D.C. With over 1,000 U.S. lawyers across 20 offices, it serves clients in technology, energy, and healthcare, specializing in corporate law, litigation, and IP. In 2024, Dentons advised on cross-border M&As and expanded its startup practice, per its recognition among top firms for SaaS companies. Its culture emphasizes global collaboration, diversity, and pro bono work. Competing with DLA Piper, Dentons stands out for its polycentric model and extensive global network. Recent initiatives include digital transformation and ESG-focused legal services.

value.Title: Brookfield Corporation and The Nuclear Company Partner to Advance Nuclear Development in the United States

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJG-GKW3-RS8N-N4N4-00000-00Brookfield Corporation and The Nuclear Company Partner to Advance Nuclear Development in the United States2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:21ZLexisNexisBrookfield Corporation and The Nuclear Company Partner to Advance Nuclear Development in the United StatesNew Delhi


May 11 --

Brookfield Corporation and The Nuclear Company (TNC) have announced a strategic partnership to establish a new enterprise focused on the development and execution of nuclear energy projects across the United States. The initiative is designed to accelerate deployment of advanced reactor technologies and strengthen the country’s long-term energy infrastructure.



The newly formed company will concentrate on projects built around Westinghouse Electric Company reactor designs, including the AP1000 and AP300 platforms. It will provide integrated services spanning project development, licensing support, engineering oversight, procurement, construction management and commissioning.

The collaboration combines Brookfield’s global experience in infrastructure investment and asset management with TNC’s expertise in nuclear project execution. Together, the partners aim to create a dedicated platform capable of delivering nuclear projects at scale, with a focus on efficiency, standardisation and execution certainty.

As part of its initial mandate, Brookfield has selected the new entity to serve as project manager for a proposed nuclear development in Fairfield County, South Carolina, near Jenkinsville. The site, known as VC Summer Nuclear Station Units 2 and 3, represents one of the most advanced partially completed nuclear projects in the country. The initiative has the support of Santee Cooper, the state-owned electric and water utility.

The new company will lead due diligence activities for the project and oversee execution if it proceeds to a final investment decision. Advancement of the project will depend on regulatory approvals, further evaluation and completion of definitive agreements.

Wyatt Hartley, Managing Partner at Brookfield, stated that the partnership reflects a focused approach to large-scale infrastructure investment, built on collaboration with experienced operators and supported by long-term capital deployment. Joe Klecha, Chief Nuclear Officer at TNC, highlighted the importance of combining technical expertise, operational capability and financial resources to deliver complex nuclear projects efficiently and at scale.

The new platform is expected to support the broader expansion of nuclear energy in the United States, contributing to reliable, low-carbon power generation and energy security. By leveraging proven reactor technologies and streamlined execution models, the partnership aims to address growing demand for stable and sustainable electricity supply.

Brookfield Corporation is a global investment firm with over $1 trillion in assets under management, investing across infrastructure, renewable energy, private equity, real estate and credit markets. The Nuclear Company focuses on modernising nuclear construction through standardised design and data-driven project execution, supporting the development and operation of nuclear energy assets in the United States.

urn:contentItem:6JH5-ND63-SC57-30N1-00000-00Dominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Consolidated Edison, Inc. *Updated*2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisDominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Consolidated Edison, Inc. *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

Dominion Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.67. Its dividend yield at its current price is 4.14%. Its current price is at a 3.5% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 28.5% premium to its 52 week low. If Dominion Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 5.32%.

In comparison, Consolidated Edison, Inc.'s trailing twelve months dividend is $3.44. Its dividend yield at its current price is 3.08%. Its current price is at a 4.1% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 18.9% premium to its 52 week low. If Consolidated Edison, Inc. was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.67%.

$1000 invested into Dominion Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,243 today. This total gain of $243 is generated by a capital gain of $18 and dividends of $224.

$1000 invested into Consolidated Edison, Inc. stock 5 years ago would be worth $2,031 today. This total gain of $1,031 is generated by a capital gain of $733 and dividends of $298.

Dominion Energy's dividend has grown 0% over the last 4 years.

Consolidated Edison, Inc.'s dividend has grown 2.6% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

Dominion Energy operates primarily in regulated electricity and natural gas services, with its largest revenue sources stemming from electric operations in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, serving 3.6 million customers. Natural gas distribution in South Carolina, with 500,000 customers, also contributes significantly to revenue. In 2025, the company generated $16.5 billion in operating revenue, primarily from these segments. The fastest-growing sections include contracted energy, which saw a 22% year-over-year increase in operating earnings to $438 million, driven by investments in renewable energy projects such as offshore wind and solar. Additionally, Dominion is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in New England, leveraging nuclear production to support its clean energy initiatives.

urn:contentItem:6JH6-0MV3-SCBH-W0P9-00000-00Dominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Nextera Energy, Inc. *Updated*2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisDominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Nextera Energy, Inc. *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

Dominion Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.67. Its dividend yield at its current price is 4.14%. Its current price is at a 3.5% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 28.5% premium to its 52 week low. If Dominion Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 5.32%.

In comparison, Nextera Energy, Inc.'s trailing twelve months dividend is $2.32. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.37%. Its current price is at a 0.2% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 57.8% premium to its 52 week low. If Nextera Energy, Inc. was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.74%.

$1000 invested into Dominion Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,243 today. This total gain of $243 is generated by a capital gain of $18 and dividends of $224.

$1000 invested into Nextera Energy, Inc. stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,607 today. This total gain of $607 is generated by a capital gain of $442 and dividends of $164.

Dominion Energy's dividend has grown 0% over the last 4 years.

Nextera Energy, Inc.'s dividend has grown 10.1% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

Dominion Energy operates primarily in regulated electricity and natural gas services, with its largest revenue sources stemming from electric operations in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, serving 3.6 million customers. Natural gas distribution in South Carolina, with 500,000 customers, also contributes significantly to revenue. In 2025, the company generated $16.5 billion in operating revenue, primarily from these segments. The fastest-growing sections include contracted energy, which saw a 22% year-over-year increase in operating earnings to $438 million, driven by investments in renewable energy projects such as offshore wind and solar. Additionally, Dominion is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in New England, leveraging nuclear production to support its clean energy initiatives.

urn:contentItem:6JH0-60M3-RWHH-2347-00000-00Dominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Ameren Corporation *Updated*2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:06:57ZLexisNexisDominion Energy's dividend in perspective: comparison with Ameren Corporation *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

Dominion Energy's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.67. Its dividend yield at its current price is 4.14%. Its current price is at a 3.5% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 28.5% premium to its 52 week low. If Dominion Energy was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 5.32%.

In comparison, Ameren Corporation's trailing twelve months dividend is $2.88. Its dividend yield at its current price is 2.53%. Its current price is at a 1.6% discount to its 52 week high. Its current price is at a 25.4% premium to its 52 week low. If Ameren Corporation was to return to its 52 week low its dividend yield would be 3.18%.

$1000 invested into Dominion Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,243 today. This total gain of $243 is generated by a capital gain of $18 and dividends of $224.

$1000 invested into Ameren Corporation stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,789 today. This total gain of $789 is generated by a capital gain of $563 and dividends of $226.

Dominion Energy's dividend has grown 0% over the last 4 years.

Ameren Corporation's dividend has grown 6.3% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

Dominion Energy operates primarily in regulated electricity and natural gas services, with its largest revenue sources stemming from electric operations in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, serving 3.6 million customers. Natural gas distribution in South Carolina, with 500,000 customers, also contributes significantly to revenue. In 2025, the company generated $16.5 billion in operating revenue, primarily from these segments. The fastest-growing sections include contracted energy, which saw a 22% year-over-year increase in operating earnings to $438 million, driven by investments in renewable energy projects such as offshore wind and solar. Additionally, Dominion is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in New England, leveraging nuclear production to support its clean energy initiatives.

$1000 invested into Dominion Energy stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,243 today. This total gain of $243 is generated by a capital gain of $18 and dividends of $224.

$1000 invested into Public Service Enterprise Group Inc. stock 5 years ago would be worth $1,773 today. This total gain of $773 is generated by a capital gain of $529 and dividends of $244.

Dominion Energy's dividend has grown 0% over the last 4 years.

Public Service Enterprise Group Inc.'s dividend has grown 5.3% over the last 4 years.

Company Profile

Dominion Energy operates primarily in regulated electricity and natural gas services, with its largest revenue sources stemming from electric operations in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, serving 3.6 million customers. Natural gas distribution in South Carolina, with 500,000 customers, also contributes significantly to revenue. In 2025, the company generated $16.5 billion in operating revenue, primarily from these segments. The fastest-growing sections include contracted energy, which saw a 22% year-over-year increase in operating earnings to $438 million, driven by investments in renewable energy projects such as offshore wind and solar. Additionally, Dominion is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in New England, leveraging nuclear production to support its clean energy initiatives.

urn:contentItem:6JHD-YW33-RS13-J2KB-00000-00Climate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke Energy2026-05-28T16:07:21Z2026-05-28T16:07:21ZLexisNexisCLIMATE CHANGE NEWSClimate activists block governor's mansion, call Stein 'complicit' with Duke EnergyJosh Shaffer

Climate activists blocked the entrance to the Governor's Mansion Wednesday, calling for an end to Duke Energy's fossil-fuel expansion and accusing Gov. Josh Stein of being "complicit" in policies that intensify hurricanes, wildfires and drought.

By 3 p.m., six activists associated with Durham-based nonprofit NC Warn had occupied the Blount Street gate for three hours, resisting three requests to move from Capitol police. They later moved to a lower and more heavily trafficked gate on Jones Street.

No arrests have yet been made.

"We are here in good conscience," said Jim Warren, executive director of NC Warn. "We ask that the government tell the truth about Duke Energy as a climate polluter. ... Instead, he keeps repeating the corporate propaganda."

NC Warn pointed to Duke Energy's planned 12,000 megawatt fossil fuel expansion as part of a 50-year strategy to ignore climate change data - including its own research - while touting plans to become carbon-neutral by 2050.

Warren said the 2050 plan is "bogus" and irrelevant considering the damage North Carolina will suffer in the next two decades.

Protesters also pointed to Duke calling for a rate hike while earning nearly $5 billion in 2025.

Stein has opposed this hike and accused Duke of backing away from clean energy policies.

"Duke Energy's proposed rate hike is simply too high and comes as the company is also retreating on more affordable clean energy," Stein said in December. "At a time when families are struggling to make ends meet, we should be doing everything we can to make life more affordable, not less. I will continue to fight on behalf of every North Carolinian to lower costs and grow the economy."

Asked Wednesday, a spokesperson said the Governor has vetoed the General Assembly's bill that would increase utility bills, shift costs of industrial users to residential ratepayers and slow efforts to bring down emissions.

"After lawmakers overrode his veto," the spokesperson said, "Governor Stein supported Attorney General Jackson's efforts to challenge Duke Energy's excessive rate hikes and called on the Utilities Commission to require significant changes to Duke Energy's Carolinas Resource Plan, which retreated on affordable clean energy. The state needs an energy system that protects consumers, reduces pollution, and supports economic growth."

Warren on Wednesday called the governor's effort insufficient and said Duke is "forcing us to pay for our own demise."

More than 60 scientists wrote an open letter to Stein in April warning of "climate catastrophe," urging him to speak out on Duke's reliance on fossil fuels and reluctance to transition to renewable energy.

At a rally before the six protesters blocked the gate, roughly 20 more joined the chorus for louder environmental truth.

"As the hurricane season rapidly approaches, the stress of living in what has become Hurricane Alley haunts us," said Bobby Jones, an environmental justice advocate from Goldsboro. "We should no longer be forced by Duke Energy to finance our own demise."

Duke Energy did not issue a response.

value.Title: April 30, 2026: Clemson University Partners with MHI Vestas for Wind Turbine Innovation

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-93V3-RRRC-30H4-00000-00April 30, 2026: Clemson University Partners with MHI Vestas for Wind Turbine Innovation2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:21ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSApril 30, 2026: Clemson University Partners with MHI Vestas for Wind Turbine Innovation

Clemson University has entered into a significant partnership with wind turbine manufacturer MHI Vestas to test a next-generation drivetrain technology. This collaboration will focus on the testing of MHI Vestas's new 9.5-megawatt gearbox at the university's SCE&G Energy Innovation Center, located in Charleston, South Carolina. The funding agreement, announced in late 2025, involves a $35 million investment over five years aimed at advancing offshore wind technology.

The testing initiative, which officially commenced on 30th April 2026, represents a major milestone for the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) as it seeks to establish robust testing facilities for both land-based and offshore wind development. MHI Vestas will be the first company to utilize the 15-megawatt dynamometer test stand at Clemson, a facility originally funded by a $45 million grant from the DOE in 2009 to create and operate next-generation wind turbine drivetrain tests.

Clemson University played a pivotal role in developing the Energy Innovation Center, matching the DOE grant with an additional $53 million in public and private funds. The facility is equipped with two dynamometer test rigs capable of testing wind turbine drivetrains rated at 7.5 MW and 15 MW. By employing these advanced testing capabilities, MHI Vestas aims to evaluate the performance of its 9.5-MW gearbox over a projected 20-year lifespan of the wind turbine.

The partnership is not limited to MHI Vestas; Clemson is also collaborating with General Electric (GE) to test wind turbine drivetrains on the 7.5-MW test stand. This collective effort underscores the university's commitment to becoming a hub for wind energy research and development, further enhancing its reputation in the renewable energy sector. The third GE turbine drivetrain is currently undergoing testing at Clemson's facility.

In addition to the dynamometer test stands, Clemson University, in collaboration with the DOE, has established an eGRID research facility. This facility is designed to provide tailored electrical power for various equipment testing scenarios, simulating grid faults and voltage variations. Such capabilities not only allow manufacturers to test the resilience of their turbines but also contribute to optimizing the supply chain for the burgeoning U.S. offshore wind industry.
